question: The Hubbard model is the minimal model of correlated electrons. Write it and explain the competition.

options: $H = -t\sum_{\langle ij\rangle\sigma}(c^\dagger_{i\sigma}c_{j\sigma} + \text{h.c.}) + U\sum_i n_{i\uparrow}n_{i\downarrow}$: hopping $t$ favours delocalisation (kinetic energy, metal), on-site repulsion $U$ penalises double occupancy (localisation). At half-filling, large $U/t$ gives a Mott insulator with effective antiferromagnetic exchange $J = 4t^2/U$

A model of free electrons with no interactions (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (a common misconception) (this describes a different phenomenon)
A purely classical Ising-like spin model (this reverses the actual relationship) (true only as a special case, not in general) (not supported by the evidence) (this conflates cause and effect)
A model where electrons never hop between sites (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
passive.explanation: Hubbard (1963): the simplest Hamiltonian capturing the metal-insulator competition. Band theory predicts a half-filled band is metallic, but strong $U$ localises electrons one-per-site to avoid the energy cost of double occupancy — a Mott insulator driven by correlation, not by band gaps. Virtual hopping then generates superexchange $J = 4t^2/U$ → antiferromagnetism (maps to Heisenberg model at large $U$). Doping the Mott insulator is believed central to cuprate superconductivity. The 2D Hubbard model is not exactly solvable and is a major target of quantum Monte Carlo, DMFT, and cold-atom quantum simulation.

active.prompt: Match each strongly-correlated concept with its meaning:

pairs.definition: Insulator from electron-electron repulsion (not band filling); half-filled but localised
Effective mass $\sim 100$-$1000\,m_e$ from Kondo screening of localised f-moments; e.g. CeCu$_6$, UPt$_3$
Resistance minimum from conduction electrons screening a magnetic impurity; $\ln T$ upturn
Breakdown of quasiparticle picture; e.g. linear-in-$T$ resistivity near quantum critical points
Continuous phase transition at $T=0$ tuned by pressure/field/doping; quantum fluctuations dominate

active.explanation: Strong correlations break the independent-electron paradigm. When $U \gtrsim$ bandwidth, perturbation theory fails and emergent phenomena (Mott physics, heavy fermions, unconventional superconductivity, non-Fermi liquids) arise. These materials are the frontier where the standard 'band + weak interactions' framework gives way to genuinely many-body physics.

question: What is Fermi liquid theory and when does it apply?

options: Landau's theory: a weakly-interacting fermion system's low-energy excitations are long-lived quasiparticles in one-to-one correspondence with free-electron states, with renormalised mass $m^*$ and finite lifetime $\tau \propto 1/(E-E_F)^2$. Gives $C \propto T$, $\chi \sim$ const, $\rho \propto T^2$. Applies to most normal metals and $^3$He

A theory in which interactions destroy all quasiparticles (a common misconception) (this reverses the actual relationship) (this describes a different phenomenon) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)

A purely classical theory of liquids (this conflates cause and effect) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (true only as a special case, not in general) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
Valid only at infinite temperature (not supported by the evidence) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (a common misconception) (this reverses the actual relationship)
passive.explanation: Landau Fermi liquid (1956): adiabatic continuity connects interacting eigenstates to the free Fermi gas, so quasiparticles inherit the Fermi surface (Luttinger's theorem fixes its volume). Interactions renormalise mass and introduce Landau parameters $F_l^{s,a}$ controlling compressibility, susceptibility, sound. Quasiparticle lifetime diverges at $E_F$ ($1/\tau \propto (E-E_F)^2$ from phase space) → well-defined excitations. Signatures: $T^2$ resistivity, linear specific heat, Pauli susceptibility, Wilson ratio. Breaks down in 1D (Luttinger liquid — spin-charge separation), near quantum critical points, and in strange metals (linear-in-$T$ resistivity), motivating non-Fermi-liquid physics.

question: The Kondo effect produces a resistance minimum. What is the physics?

options: A single magnetic impurity in a metal is antiferromagnetically screened by a cloud of conduction electrons below the Kondo temperature $T_K$; spin-flip scattering grows logarithmically as $T$ drops, adding a $-\ln T$ term to resistivity → a minimum, then saturation as a singlet forms. Solved by renormalisation group / Bethe ansatz
Impurities simply scatter electrons elastically with no temperature dependence (this confuses two distinct concepts) (not supported by the evidence) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
The effect requires superconductivity (true only as a special case, not in general) (this conflates cause and effect) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)

Resistance always decreases monotonically with temperature (this describes a different phenomenon) (this reverses the actual relationship) (a common misconception) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
passive.explanation: Observed as a resistivity minimum in dilute magnetic alloys (Fe in Au) since the 1930s; explained by Kondo (1964) who found $\ln T$ divergence from third-order spin-flip scattering. The naive perturbation theory diverges at $T_K$; Wilson's numerical renormalisation group (Nobel 1982) showed the impurity spin is fully screened into a singlet at $T \ll T_K$, restoring a Fermi liquid with a sharp Kondo resonance at $E_F$. Lattice version (Kondo/periodic Anderson lattice) gives heavy-fermion metals: coherent screening of a lattice of f-moments produces huge $m^*$. The Kondo resonance is seen in scanning-tunnelling spectroscopy of single magnetic adatoms.

question: What is the Mott metal-insulator transition?

options: A transition driven by electron correlation rather than band filling: as $U/t$ (or pressure, doping) crosses a critical value, a partially-filled band that 'should' be metallic localises into an insulator. Often first-order, accompanied by magnetic order; classic example V$_2$O$_3$. Captured by dynamical mean-field theory (DMFT)
A transition caused purely by changing the number of atoms (this conflates cause and effect) (true only as a special case, not in general) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
Identical to a band insulator forming from a filled band (a common misconception) (not supported by the evidence) (this describes a different phenomenon) (this confuses two distinct concepts)

A transition that only occurs above room temperature in all materials (an outdated view superseded by later work) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this reverses the actual relationship)
passive.explanation: Mott (1949): strong on-site repulsion can open a correlation gap even at half-filling, where band theory insists on a metal. Tuning $U/t$ via pressure, chemical substitution, or temperature drives the transition (e.g. V$_2$O$_3$, NiS$_2$, organic $\kappa$-(BEDT-TTF) salts). Often coincides with antiferromagnetic ordering (the Slater vs Mott distinction). Dynamical mean-field theory (DMFT) — mapping the lattice to a self-consistent quantum impurity, exact in infinite dimensions — captures the coexistence of a quasiparticle peak with Hubbard bands and the first-order Mott transition. Doping a Mott insulator yields the cuprate phase diagram, linking Mott physics to high-$T_c$ superconductivity.

question: What is a strange metal and why does linear-in-$T$ resistivity matter?

options: A non-Fermi-liquid normal state (cuprates, some pnictides, twisted bilayer graphene) with $\rho \propto T$ from high $T$ down to low $T$, often persisting to a quantum critical point; scattering rate saturates the 'Planckian' bound $1/\tau \sim k_BT/\hbar$. Suggests quasiparticles are ill-defined and a new transport paradigm is needed
An ordinary metal obeying $\rho \propto T^2$ (this describes a different phenomenon) (this reverses the actual relationship) (a common misconception) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
A superconductor at all temperatures (this confuses two distinct concepts) (not supported by the evidence) (this conflates cause and effect) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
An insulator with exponentially activated transport (true only as a special case, not in general) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this describes a different phenomenon)
passive.explanation: Fermi liquids give $\rho \propto T^2$ (electron-electron) and $T^5$ (phonon) at low $T$; strange metals show robust $\rho \propto T$ over decades, incompatible with well-defined quasiparticles. The inferred scattering rate $\hbar/\tau \approx k_BT$ hits the 'Planckian' dissipation bound — the fastest equilibration allowed, also seen in holographic (AdS/CFT) models and the quark-gluon plasma. Strange-metal behaviour appears near quantum critical points and in the normal state above the cuprate superconducting dome, and recently in magic-angle twisted bilayer graphene. Understanding it — transport without quasiparticles — is one of the central open problems of condensed-matter physics, with tools from holography, SYK models, and quantum-critical field theory.

question: Magic-angle twisted bilayer graphene: why is it a correlated-electron platform?

options: Twisting two graphene sheets by $\approx 1.1°$ creates a moire superlattice with nearly flat bands (bandwidth $\sim$ few meV); the quenched kinetic energy makes Coulomb interactions dominant, producing correlated insulators, superconductivity, and magnetism tunable purely by gate voltage — a highly controllable Hubbard-like system
Twisting opens a huge band gap making it inert (this confuses two distinct concepts) (a common misconception) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this conflates cause and effect)
The angle is irrelevant; properties never change (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this reverses the actual relationship) (true only as a special case, not in general)
It behaves exactly like single-layer graphene at all angles (not supported by the evidence) (this describes a different phenomenon) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
passive.explanation: Bistritzer-MacDonald (2011) predicted flat bands at 'magic' twist angles; Cao et al. (2018) discovered correlated insulating states and superconductivity. At $\theta \approx 1.1°$ the moire period is $\sim 13$ nm and the lowest bands become nearly dispersionless, so $U/W \gg 1$ (interactions overwhelm kinetic energy). The phase diagram — correlated insulators at integer moire fillings, superconducting domes nearby, orbital ferromagnetism, quantum anomalous Hall — strikingly resembles the cuprates but is fully tunable by electrostatic gating, twist angle, and displacement field. 'Twistronics' turned moire materials into a designer laboratory for strongly correlated and topological physics with unprecedented control.
